channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #438
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Growing Concerns Over Rental Affordability for Older Tenants

Recent reports highlight an increasing proportion of older renters facing financial strain, with many spending over a third of their income on housing costs. For those on the lowest incomes, this figure rises to nearly half (48%) of their earnings. Housing campaign group Independent Age has urged the government to reassess Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rates, which have remained frozen for two years despite rents rising by 10.5% in the same period.

The current situation has led to calls for intervention, as the Renters’ Reform Act does not directly address rental affordability. With LHA failing to keep pace with market increases, campaigners argue that vulnerable tenants – particularly pensioners and low-income households – are being priced out of stable accommodation.

Landlords Face Critical Deadline for Tenant Documentation

Private landlords are being reminded of their legal obligation to provide tenants with the government’s new Information Sheet under the Renters’ Reform Act before the 31st May deadline. Failure to comply could result in penalties of up to £7,000 per property.

Industry experts have noted confusion surrounding responsibility for serving these documents, particularly where letting agents are involved. Government guidance states that managing agents must distribute the Information Sheet, but does not clarify whether landlords should duplicate this process if their agent has already acted.

Property professionals suggest:

  • Self-managing landlords should serve the documents immediately if not already done
  • Those using agents should confirm compliance in writing before the deadline
  • Where agent compliance is uncertain, landlords may wish to serve documents themselves as precaution

With the housing sector undergoing significant regulatory changes, both tenants and landlords face challenges adapting to new requirements. While affordability pressures continue for renters, property owners must navigate evolving compliance landscapes to avoid substantial penalties.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Growing Concerns Over Rental Affordability for Older Tenants

Recent reports highlight an increasing proportion of older renters facing financial strain, with many spending over a third of their income on housing costs. For those on the lowest incomes, this figure rises to nearly half (48%) of their earnings. Housing campaign group Independent Age has urged the government to reassess Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rates, which have remained frozen for two years despite rents rising by 10.5% in the same period.

The current situation has led to calls for intervention, as the Renters’ Reform Act does not directly address rental affordability. With LHA failing to keep pace with market increases, campaigners argue that vulnerable tenants – particularly pensioners and low-income households – are being priced out of stable accommodation.

Landlords Face Critical Deadline for Tenant Documentation

Private landlords are being reminded of their legal obligation to provide tenants with the government’s new Information Sheet under the Renters’ Reform Act before the 31st May deadline. Failure to comply could result in penalties of up to £7,000 per property.

Industry experts have noted confusion surrounding responsibility for serving these documents, particularly where letting agents are involved. Government guidance states that managing agents must distribute the Information Sheet, but does not clarify whether landlords should duplicate this process if their agent has already acted.

Property professionals suggest:

  • Self-managing landlords should serve the documents immediately if not already done
  • Those using agents should confirm compliance in writing before the deadline
  • Where agent compliance is uncertain, landlords may wish to serve documents themselves as precaution

With the housing sector undergoing significant regulatory changes, both tenants and landlords face challenges adapting to new requirements. While affordability pressures continue for renters, property owners must navigate evolving compliance landscapes to avoid substantial penalties.

item.title: What is currently happening in the UK property market?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market continues to show resilience in 2026, with recent data revealing surprising strength in sales activity despite broader economic challenges. Week 20 saw 27,200 homes sold subject to contract – marking the second strongest week for transactions this year. This performance sits comfortably above both the 2026 weekly average (24,900) and the decade-long benchmark for this period (25,300).

Market Activity Defies Expectations

New property listings remain robust, with 39,600 homes entering the market in Week 20. Whilst slightly below the previous week's 41,800, this figure exceeds both the current year's average (37,600) and the 10-year seasonal norm (34,900). Year-to-date listings stand at 753,000 – marginally ahead of 2025 levels and significantly above pre-pandemic averages.

Price adjustments tell an interesting story, with 27,200 reductions recorded across the 731,000 properties currently on the market. The proportion of discounted homes has stabilised around 13% in recent months, remaining above both last year's average (12.8%) and the long-term trend (10.7%). This suggests sellers are becoming more realistic about pricing in the current climate.

Regional Spotlight: Crystal Palace Case Study

Beyond national trends, a recent deep-dive into Crystal Palace's property market offers valuable insights for both buyers and sellers. The analysis examined agent performance metrics including market share, valuation accuracy, and transaction times – factors that could help homeowners identify the best representatives for their sale.

The research highlights how some agents continue to overvalue properties to win instructions, only to subsequently reduce prices. Savvy sellers might consider examining an agent's track record on:

  • Percentage of initial asking prices achieved
  • Average time from listing to sale agreed
  • Fall-through rates compared to local competitors

These indicators often prove more revealing than headline commission rates when selecting an estate agent.

As the market evolves, both buyers and sellers may benefit from looking beyond national headlines to understand hyper-local dynamics. With sales volumes holding up better than many predicted and pricing becoming more nuanced, careful research could prove invaluable for those navigating today's property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market continues to show resilience in 2026, with recent data revealing surprising strength in sales activity despite broader economic challenges. Week 20 saw 27,200 homes sold subject to contract – marking the second strongest week for transactions this year. This performance sits comfortably above both the 2026 weekly average (24,900) and the decade-long benchmark for this period (25,300).

Market Activity Defies Expectations

New property listings remain robust, with 39,600 homes entering the market in Week 20. Whilst slightly below the previous week's 41,800, this figure exceeds both the current year's average (37,600) and the 10-year seasonal norm (34,900). Year-to-date listings stand at 753,000 – marginally ahead of 2025 levels and significantly above pre-pandemic averages.

Price adjustments tell an interesting story, with 27,200 reductions recorded across the 731,000 properties currently on the market. The proportion of discounted homes has stabilised around 13% in recent months, remaining above both last year's average (12.8%) and the long-term trend (10.7%). This suggests sellers are becoming more realistic about pricing in the current climate.

Regional Spotlight: Crystal Palace Case Study

Beyond national trends, a recent deep-dive into Crystal Palace's property market offers valuable insights for both buyers and sellers. The analysis examined agent performance metrics including market share, valuation accuracy, and transaction times – factors that could help homeowners identify the best representatives for their sale.

The research highlights how some agents continue to overvalue properties to win instructions, only to subsequently reduce prices. Savvy sellers might consider examining an agent's track record on:

  • Percentage of initial asking prices achieved
  • Average time from listing to sale agreed
  • Fall-through rates compared to local competitors

These indicators often prove more revealing than headline commission rates when selecting an estate agent.

As the market evolves, both buyers and sellers may benefit from looking beyond national headlines to understand hyper-local dynamics. With sales volumes holding up better than many predicted and pricing becoming more nuanced, careful research could prove invaluable for those navigating today's property landscape.

item.title: Rental sector faces increased scrutiny under new government data strategy
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government has announced plans to intensify data collection in the private rented sector, aiming to closely monitor the effects of the landmark Renters’ Rights Act. This new strategy seeks to create a comprehensive evidence base to assess whether recent reforms are delivering tangible improvements for tenants whilst maintaining a balanced market for landlords.

Enhanced Monitoring of Rental Market Reforms

Under the proposals, officials will combine existing survey data with fresh information from the Private Rented Sector Database and the PRS Ombudsman. The initiative forms part of a broader effort to evaluate the impact of what ministers describe as the most significant rental sector reforms in decades.

Key areas under scrutiny include:

  • Tenancy security and eviction rates
  • Rental affordability trends
  • Compliance with housing standards
  • Accessibility of rental properties
  • Effectiveness of dispute resolution systems

Long-Term Evaluation Framework

The data strategy outlines a multi-year evaluation process, with findings scheduled for publication in 2028 and 2031 – two and five years respectively after the Renters’ Rights Act implementation. This extended timeframe aims to provide meaningful insights into how the reforms affect behaviours across the sector.

Policymakers suggest the enhanced data framework could help identify where additional interventions might be needed, whilst enabling more transparent reporting to Parliament. The government intends to share appropriate datasets publicly to support research and informed debate about rental market dynamics.

For landlords and letting agents, the strategy signals increased transparency requirements, though officials stress the importance of proportionate reporting that doesn’t create unnecessary administrative burdens. Tenants might benefit from better-informed policies addressing persistent challenges around housing quality and affordability.

This data-led approach reflects growing emphasis on evidence-based housing policy, with the government aiming to balance tenant protections with a sustainable private rental market. The coming years will reveal whether these monitoring efforts translate into measurable improvements for England’s 11 million private renters.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government has announced plans to intensify data collection in the private rented sector, aiming to closely monitor the effects of the landmark Renters’ Rights Act. This new strategy seeks to create a comprehensive evidence base to assess whether recent reforms are delivering tangible improvements for tenants whilst maintaining a balanced market for landlords.

Enhanced Monitoring of Rental Market Reforms

Under the proposals, officials will combine existing survey data with fresh information from the Private Rented Sector Database and the PRS Ombudsman. The initiative forms part of a broader effort to evaluate the impact of what ministers describe as the most significant rental sector reforms in decades.

Key areas under scrutiny include:

  • Tenancy security and eviction rates
  • Rental affordability trends
  • Compliance with housing standards
  • Accessibility of rental properties
  • Effectiveness of dispute resolution systems

Long-Term Evaluation Framework

The data strategy outlines a multi-year evaluation process, with findings scheduled for publication in 2028 and 2031 – two and five years respectively after the Renters’ Rights Act implementation. This extended timeframe aims to provide meaningful insights into how the reforms affect behaviours across the sector.

Policymakers suggest the enhanced data framework could help identify where additional interventions might be needed, whilst enabling more transparent reporting to Parliament. The government intends to share appropriate datasets publicly to support research and informed debate about rental market dynamics.

For landlords and letting agents, the strategy signals increased transparency requirements, though officials stress the importance of proportionate reporting that doesn’t create unnecessary administrative burdens. Tenants might benefit from better-informed policies addressing persistent challenges around housing quality and affordability.

This data-led approach reflects growing emphasis on evidence-based housing policy, with the government aiming to balance tenant protections with a sustainable private rental market. The coming years will reveal whether these monitoring efforts translate into measurable improvements for England’s 11 million private renters.

item.title: KFH moves away from annual charges in landlord fee shake-up
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Kinleigh Folkard & Hayward (KFH), one of London's largest lettings agencies, has announced a significant shift in its fee structure for landlords. Moving away from traditional upfront charges, the firm will now implement a monthly payment model, aiming to ease financial pressures on property investors amidst evolving rental market regulations.

New Fee Structure Aims to Support Landlords

The agency, which operates over 50 branches across the capital, stated that the change is designed to improve cash flow for landlords while adapting to the requirements of the Renters’ Rights Act. With the private rented sector facing increased compliance obligations, KFH's revised approach could offer greater flexibility for property owners navigating regulatory changes.

John Ennis, chief revenue officer at Lomond, commented: "London property remains one of the strongest long-term investments in the UK, and our services should reflect how the market is evolving. The Renters’ Rights Act makes tenancies periodic, so it’s only right our fees move to a monthly model too."

London Rental Market Shows Resilience

The announcement coincides with KFH's latest market research, revealing continued strength in London's rental sector:

  • Average rents increased by 1.5% in 2025
  • Supply of rental homes grew by 3% year-on-year

Ennis added: "This isn’t just a pricing update. It’s a signal of how seriously we take landlord partnerships. The monthly model is designed to make best-in-class property management more accessible than ever."

Landlords considering their options might find the new fee structure worth exploring, particularly those looking to improve cash flow whilst maintaining professional property management services. As the rental market continues to adapt to legislative changes, such flexible approaches may become increasingly common across the sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Kinleigh Folkard & Hayward (KFH), one of London's largest lettings agencies, has announced a significant shift in its fee structure for landlords. Moving away from traditional upfront charges, the firm will now implement a monthly payment model, aiming to ease financial pressures on property investors amidst evolving rental market regulations.

New Fee Structure Aims to Support Landlords

The agency, which operates over 50 branches across the capital, stated that the change is designed to improve cash flow for landlords while adapting to the requirements of the Renters’ Rights Act. With the private rented sector facing increased compliance obligations, KFH's revised approach could offer greater flexibility for property owners navigating regulatory changes.

John Ennis, chief revenue officer at Lomond, commented: "London property remains one of the strongest long-term investments in the UK, and our services should reflect how the market is evolving. The Renters’ Rights Act makes tenancies periodic, so it’s only right our fees move to a monthly model too."

London Rental Market Shows Resilience

The announcement coincides with KFH's latest market research, revealing continued strength in London's rental sector:

  • Average rents increased by 1.5% in 2025
  • Supply of rental homes grew by 3% year-on-year

Ennis added: "This isn’t just a pricing update. It’s a signal of how seriously we take landlord partnerships. The monthly model is designed to make best-in-class property management more accessible than ever."

Landlords considering their options might find the new fee structure worth exploring, particularly those looking to improve cash flow whilst maintaining professional property management services. As the rental market continues to adapt to legislative changes, such flexible approaches may become increasingly common across the sector.

item.title: Property Franchise Group secures strong shareholder support for growth plans
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property Franchise Group Gains Shareholder Backing for Expansion Plans

The Property Franchise Group (TPFG) has secured strong shareholder support for its strategic direction following its recent annual general meeting. Investors overwhelmingly approved all 17 resolutions, including the adoption of annual accounts, director re-elections, dividend payments and auditor reappointments. The votes grant the company continued flexibility for share issuances, buybacks and acquisitions as it pursues further growth in the property franchising sector.

Resilient Business Model Amid Market Challenges

TPFG's trading update revealed the group continues to perform in line with board expectations, benefiting from a cash-generative business model with substantial recurring revenues. This income stream provides stability against housing market fluctuations while supporting franchisees across its network of nearly 2,000 outlets. The group operates well-known brands including Belvoir, Hunters and Martin & Co, covering residential sales, lettings and financial services.

While most resolutions passed with minimal opposition, approximately 14% of shareholders voted against the directors' remuneration policy. However, the measure still achieved comfortable approval, indicating limited widespread concern over executive pay arrangements.

Regulatory Changes Create New Opportunities

The board highlighted the forthcoming Renters' Rights Act (2026) as a potential growth driver, anticipating increased demand from landlords needing professional compliance support. TPFG reports rising enquiries from self-managed landlords seeking assistance with regulatory requirements, suggesting the legislation may accelerate adoption of managed property services.

This regulatory shift aligns with TPFG's ongoing platform development strategy, which has seen steady progress according to the update. The group's established infrastructure positions it to capitalise on market changes whilst maintaining service standards across its national network.

With shareholder backing secured and market conditions creating new opportunities, TPFG appears well-placed to continue its expansion in the UK property sector. The combination of recurring revenues, regulatory tailwinds and strategic flexibility suggests the franchising specialist could maintain its growth trajectory in coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property Franchise Group Gains Shareholder Backing for Expansion Plans

The Property Franchise Group (TPFG) has secured strong shareholder support for its strategic direction following its recent annual general meeting. Investors overwhelmingly approved all 17 resolutions, including the adoption of annual accounts, director re-elections, dividend payments and auditor reappointments. The votes grant the company continued flexibility for share issuances, buybacks and acquisitions as it pursues further growth in the property franchising sector.

Resilient Business Model Amid Market Challenges

TPFG's trading update revealed the group continues to perform in line with board expectations, benefiting from a cash-generative business model with substantial recurring revenues. This income stream provides stability against housing market fluctuations while supporting franchisees across its network of nearly 2,000 outlets. The group operates well-known brands including Belvoir, Hunters and Martin & Co, covering residential sales, lettings and financial services.

While most resolutions passed with minimal opposition, approximately 14% of shareholders voted against the directors' remuneration policy. However, the measure still achieved comfortable approval, indicating limited widespread concern over executive pay arrangements.

Regulatory Changes Create New Opportunities

The board highlighted the forthcoming Renters' Rights Act (2026) as a potential growth driver, anticipating increased demand from landlords needing professional compliance support. TPFG reports rising enquiries from self-managed landlords seeking assistance with regulatory requirements, suggesting the legislation may accelerate adoption of managed property services.

This regulatory shift aligns with TPFG's ongoing platform development strategy, which has seen steady progress according to the update. The group's established infrastructure positions it to capitalise on market changes whilst maintaining service standards across its national network.

With shareholder backing secured and market conditions creating new opportunities, TPFG appears well-placed to continue its expansion in the UK property sector. The combination of recurring revenues, regulatory tailwinds and strategic flexibility suggests the franchising specialist could maintain its growth trajectory in coming years.

item.title: Property industry reacts to Zoopla house price data
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The latest Zoopla House Price Index has revealed a housing market that continues to display resilience, despite mixed signals. Whilst buyer demand has softened by 10% compared to last year, agreed sales remain 1% higher, indicating that essential movers are still transacting. Meanwhile, discretionary buyers appear more cautious, with higher borrowing costs and economic uncertainty influencing their decisions. This nuanced picture suggests a market that is adapting to current challenges whilst maintaining steady activity.

Stock Levels Rise as Buyers Gain More Choice

One notable trend in the Zoopla data is the increase in housing stock, with the number of homes coming onto the market rising by 3.4% year-on-year. This growth in supply is giving buyers greater choice and could help to balance the market, particularly in areas where demand has been high. For homeowners looking to sell, this means pricing strategies may need to be more competitive to attract interest. Meanwhile, buyers might find themselves in a stronger position to negotiate, especially in regions where stock levels are rising more significantly.

Regional Variations in House Price Growth

House price inflation across the UK currently stands at 1.5%, but the report highlights significant regional variations. The strongest growth continues to be seen in more affordable markets, particularly in northern England, Scotland, and Wales, where price increases range from 2% to 3.6%. This contrasts with slower growth in higher-value areas, reflecting ongoing affordability pressures. For first-time buyers, these regions may offer more accessible opportunities, especially as budgets have risen by 4.3% compared to last year. This suggests that committed movers are prioritising long-term lifestyle needs over short-term market fluctuations.

Industry Insights on Market Resilience

Industry experts have noted the resilience of the housing market, with Iain McKenzie, CEO of The Guild of Property Professionals, highlighting the determination of first-time buyers. He pointed out that whilst overall demand has softened, the increase in agreed sales demonstrates that many buyers are still moving forward with their plans. Factors such as growing families, job relocations, and other life events are driving this activity, even amidst economic uncertainty.

McKenzie also emphasised the improving mortgage approval rates and faster selling times compared to earlier in the year. With mortgage rates stabilising and inflation easing, lenders are becoming more competitive, which is helping to bolster confidence across the market. However, buyers are increasingly price-sensitive and selective, meaning sellers may need to be realistic about pricing to secure a sale.

Overall, the Zoopla House Price Index paints a picture of a housing market that is holding steady, albeit with challenges. For those considering a move, staying informed about regional trends and market dynamics could prove invaluable in making well-timed decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The latest Zoopla House Price Index has revealed a housing market that continues to display resilience, despite mixed signals. Whilst buyer demand has softened by 10% compared to last year, agreed sales remain 1% higher, indicating that essential movers are still transacting. Meanwhile, discretionary buyers appear more cautious, with higher borrowing costs and economic uncertainty influencing their decisions. This nuanced picture suggests a market that is adapting to current challenges whilst maintaining steady activity.

Stock Levels Rise as Buyers Gain More Choice

One notable trend in the Zoopla data is the increase in housing stock, with the number of homes coming onto the market rising by 3.4% year-on-year. This growth in supply is giving buyers greater choice and could help to balance the market, particularly in areas where demand has been high. For homeowners looking to sell, this means pricing strategies may need to be more competitive to attract interest. Meanwhile, buyers might find themselves in a stronger position to negotiate, especially in regions where stock levels are rising more significantly.

Regional Variations in House Price Growth

House price inflation across the UK currently stands at 1.5%, but the report highlights significant regional variations. The strongest growth continues to be seen in more affordable markets, particularly in northern England, Scotland, and Wales, where price increases range from 2% to 3.6%. This contrasts with slower growth in higher-value areas, reflecting ongoing affordability pressures. For first-time buyers, these regions may offer more accessible opportunities, especially as budgets have risen by 4.3% compared to last year. This suggests that committed movers are prioritising long-term lifestyle needs over short-term market fluctuations.

Industry Insights on Market Resilience

Industry experts have noted the resilience of the housing market, with Iain McKenzie, CEO of The Guild of Property Professionals, highlighting the determination of first-time buyers. He pointed out that whilst overall demand has softened, the increase in agreed sales demonstrates that many buyers are still moving forward with their plans. Factors such as growing families, job relocations, and other life events are driving this activity, even amidst economic uncertainty.

McKenzie also emphasised the improving mortgage approval rates and faster selling times compared to earlier in the year. With mortgage rates stabilising and inflation easing, lenders are becoming more competitive, which is helping to bolster confidence across the market. However, buyers are increasingly price-sensitive and selective, meaning sellers may need to be realistic about pricing to secure a sale.

Overall, the Zoopla House Price Index paints a picture of a housing market that is holding steady, albeit with challenges. For those considering a move, staying informed about regional trends and market dynamics could prove invaluable in making well-timed decisions.

item.title: Government threatened with major legal action over property reforms
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~
item.content:encoded: 关于这个问题，我没有相关信息，您可以尝试问我其它问题，我会尽力为您解答~

item.title: First-time buyer scheme to relaunch next month, government confirms
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Scottish government has announced the return of the First Home Fund, with the scheme set to reopen by the end of June. The initiative, which provides deposit assistance for first-time buyers, comes as affordability challenges persist across Scotland’s property market, particularly in high-value areas like Edinburgh and East Lothian.

What the relaunched scheme offers

Under the revised First Home Fund, eligible buyers purchasing properties worth up to £300,000 could receive up to £10,000 towards their deposit. The government aims to support around 50,000 first-time buyers over the next five years through the programme. While the property value threshold has increased since the scheme’s previous iteration in 2021, the maximum financial support has been reduced from £25,000 to £10,000 per applicant.

Property portal ESPC, which campaigned for the scheme’s return, welcomed the announcement but noted the lower funding levels might limit the scheme’s effectiveness in more expensive markets. Paul Hilton, ESPC’s CEO, highlighted that while the reduced support allows the fund to help more buyers overall, individual purchasers in high-price areas may find the assistance less impactful given recent house price growth.

Addressing first-time buyer challenges

The return of the First Home Fund follows significant demand for previous government-backed schemes. In 2021, the original version exhausted its annual budget within eight days, while its successor, the Open Market Shared Equity (OMSE) scheme, reached capacity within a month. These figures underscore the ongoing need for first-time buyer support, particularly in regions where property prices outpace wage growth.

Prospective homeowners might consider reviewing all available support options when planning their property purchase. Alongside the First Home Fund, other schemes such as LISAs and Help to Buy may provide alternative routes onto the property ladder, depending on individual circumstances and location.

As the Scottish property market continues to evolve, the relaunch of the First Home Fund represents another tool for first-time buyers navigating today’s challenging housing landscape. With applications opening shortly, those considering the scheme may wish to research eligibility criteria and prepare necessary documentation in advance.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Scottish government has announced the return of the First Home Fund, with the scheme set to reopen by the end of June. The initiative, which provides deposit assistance for first-time buyers, comes as affordability challenges persist across Scotland’s property market, particularly in high-value areas like Edinburgh and East Lothian.

What the relaunched scheme offers

Under the revised First Home Fund, eligible buyers purchasing properties worth up to £300,000 could receive up to £10,000 towards their deposit. The government aims to support around 50,000 first-time buyers over the next five years through the programme. While the property value threshold has increased since the scheme’s previous iteration in 2021, the maximum financial support has been reduced from £25,000 to £10,000 per applicant.

Property portal ESPC, which campaigned for the scheme’s return, welcomed the announcement but noted the lower funding levels might limit the scheme’s effectiveness in more expensive markets. Paul Hilton, ESPC’s CEO, highlighted that while the reduced support allows the fund to help more buyers overall, individual purchasers in high-price areas may find the assistance less impactful given recent house price growth.

Addressing first-time buyer challenges

The return of the First Home Fund follows significant demand for previous government-backed schemes. In 2021, the original version exhausted its annual budget within eight days, while its successor, the Open Market Shared Equity (OMSE) scheme, reached capacity within a month. These figures underscore the ongoing need for first-time buyer support, particularly in regions where property prices outpace wage growth.

Prospective homeowners might consider reviewing all available support options when planning their property purchase. Alongside the First Home Fund, other schemes such as LISAs and Help to Buy may provide alternative routes onto the property ladder, depending on individual circumstances and location.

As the Scottish property market continues to evolve, the relaunch of the First Home Fund represents another tool for first-time buyers navigating today’s challenging housing landscape. With applications opening shortly, those considering the scheme may wish to research eligibility criteria and prepare necessary documentation in advance.

item.title: Housing market holds steady as first-time buyers spend more
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Despite rising mortgage costs and economic uncertainty, first-time buyers in the UK are demonstrating resilience by targeting higher-value properties, according to the latest Zoopla House Price Index. The report reveals that the average home sought by first-time buyers now stands at £254,750, marking a 4.3% increase compared to last year. This growth rate significantly outpaces the wider UK house price inflation, which currently sits at 1.5%, with the average property valued at £271,900.

First-Time Buyers Prioritise Larger Properties

The data highlights that first-time buyers are increasingly focusing on larger or more expensive homes, even amidst affordability pressures. This trend is particularly pronounced in London, where the average property targeted by first-time buyers has surpassed £500,000 for the first time, reaching £502,250. Despite this, overall house prices in the capital remain relatively stable, giving buyers greater negotiating power as the number of homes listed for sale continues to rise.

Outside London, more affordable regions such as northern England, Scotland, and Wales are experiencing stronger house price growth, with increases ranging between 2% and 3.6%. This growth is supported by improving mortgage affordability and lower levels of available stock, making these areas particularly attractive to buyers.

Market Activity Shows Signs of Recovery

The wider housing market is also showing tentative signs of recovery, with sales agreed currently running 1% ahead of last year’s figures. This marks the first positive sales figure recorded in 2026, suggesting that committed movers are continuing to transact despite a 10% decline in overall buyer demand. Zoopla attributes this to more cautious or discretionary buyers remaining on the sidelines, whilst those determined to move are proceeding with their plans.

The number of new homes coming onto the market has increased by 3.4% year-on-year, helping to sustain sales activity even as demand weakens. This boost in supply could provide buyers with more options and potentially ease some of the upward pressure on prices in certain regions.

As the market continues to evolve, homeowners may wish to monitor these trends closely to make informed decisions about their next steps. With affordability challenges persisting, it could be worth exploring the options available in more affordable regions or considering the potential benefits of increased negotiating power in areas with rising stock levels.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Despite rising mortgage costs and economic uncertainty, first-time buyers in the UK are demonstrating resilience by targeting higher-value properties, according to the latest Zoopla House Price Index. The report reveals that the average home sought by first-time buyers now stands at £254,750, marking a 4.3% increase compared to last year. This growth rate significantly outpaces the wider UK house price inflation, which currently sits at 1.5%, with the average property valued at £271,900.

First-Time Buyers Prioritise Larger Properties

The data highlights that first-time buyers are increasingly focusing on larger or more expensive homes, even amidst affordability pressures. This trend is particularly pronounced in London, where the average property targeted by first-time buyers has surpassed £500,000 for the first time, reaching £502,250. Despite this, overall house prices in the capital remain relatively stable, giving buyers greater negotiating power as the number of homes listed for sale continues to rise.

Outside London, more affordable regions such as northern England, Scotland, and Wales are experiencing stronger house price growth, with increases ranging between 2% and 3.6%. This growth is supported by improving mortgage affordability and lower levels of available stock, making these areas particularly attractive to buyers.

Market Activity Shows Signs of Recovery

The wider housing market is also showing tentative signs of recovery, with sales agreed currently running 1% ahead of last year’s figures. This marks the first positive sales figure recorded in 2026, suggesting that committed movers are continuing to transact despite a 10% decline in overall buyer demand. Zoopla attributes this to more cautious or discretionary buyers remaining on the sidelines, whilst those determined to move are proceeding with their plans.

The number of new homes coming onto the market has increased by 3.4% year-on-year, helping to sustain sales activity even as demand weakens. This boost in supply could provide buyers with more options and potentially ease some of the upward pressure on prices in certain regions.

As the market continues to evolve, homeowners may wish to monitor these trends closely to make informed decisions about their next steps. With affordability challenges persisting, it could be worth exploring the options available in more affordable regions or considering the potential benefits of increased negotiating power in areas with rising stock levels.

item.title: First-time buyers aiming higher
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

First-time buyers appear to be setting their sights on pricier properties, even as the housing market shows signs of cooling amidst broader economic uncertainty. This emerging trend suggests shifting priorities amongst new entrants to the property ladder.

Changing first-time buyer preferences

Recent market observations indicate that first-time purchasers might be prioritising larger homes or more desirable locations compared to previous years. Several factors could be influencing this shift:

  • The prolonged period of remote working arrangements may have increased demand for properties with home office space
  • Changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic could be making buyers reconsider what they want from a home
  • Competitive mortgage products tailored for first-time buyers might be enabling higher borrowing

Market conditions and affordability considerations

Whilst this trend towards more expensive properties emerges, prospective buyers would be wise to carefully assess their financial position. The current economic climate presents both opportunities and challenges:

  • Some areas have seen modest price corrections, potentially creating buying opportunities
  • Interest rate fluctuations continue to impact mortgage affordability calculations
  • Government schemes like Help to Buy and shared ownership remain available to eligible applicants

First-time buyers considering higher-value properties may wish to thoroughly research their mortgage options and consult with independent financial advisers. The market's current dynamics underscore the importance of careful financial planning when making one of life's most significant purchases.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

First-time buyers appear to be setting their sights on pricier properties, even as the housing market shows signs of cooling amidst broader economic uncertainty. This emerging trend suggests shifting priorities amongst new entrants to the property ladder.

Changing first-time buyer preferences

Recent market observations indicate that first-time purchasers might be prioritising larger homes or more desirable locations compared to previous years. Several factors could be influencing this shift:

  • The prolonged period of remote working arrangements may have increased demand for properties with home office space
  • Changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic could be making buyers reconsider what they want from a home
  • Competitive mortgage products tailored for first-time buyers might be enabling higher borrowing

Market conditions and affordability considerations

Whilst this trend towards more expensive properties emerges, prospective buyers would be wise to carefully assess their financial position. The current economic climate presents both opportunities and challenges:

  • Some areas have seen modest price corrections, potentially creating buying opportunities
  • Interest rate fluctuations continue to impact mortgage affordability calculations
  • Government schemes like Help to Buy and shared ownership remain available to eligible applicants

First-time buyers considering higher-value properties may wish to thoroughly research their mortgage options and consult with independent financial advisers. The market's current dynamics underscore the importance of careful financial planning when making one of life's most significant purchases.

item.title: Need Help Understanding the Renters Rights Act? Here Are Your Training Options
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Understanding the Renters Reform Act: Training Options Available

With the Renters Reform Act now in effect, landlords, letting agents and housing professionals may wish to ensure they're fully informed about the changing legal requirements. Several training options exist to help stakeholders navigate these reforms, ranging from free introductory courses to bespoke in-house sessions.

Free and Paid Learning Resources

For those new to renting regulations or needing a refresher, a free 12-part online course covers essential landlord and tenant law. More comprehensive training comes through a series of 16 monthly webinars addressing multiple aspects of property letting, including:

  • Pre-tenancy preparations
  • Property standards compliance
  • Local authority enforcement procedures
  • Tenancy agreement best practices
  • Possession proceedings guidance

These live sessions allow for participant questions and offer recorded access for twelve months afterwards. While included in certain membership packages, non-members might consider paying £18 per webinar, with continuing professional development certificates available upon request.

Specialist Training Solutions

A three-hour masterclass recording remains accessible through business membership portals, detailing all Renters Reform Act changes. Organisations requiring tailored training might explore customised programmes delivered either by housing law specialists or qualified barristers.

From fundamental courses to specialist sessions, these learning opportunities could help property professionals maintain compliance and confidence during this period of legislative change.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Understanding the Renters Reform Act: Training Options Available

With the Renters Reform Act now in effect, landlords, letting agents and housing professionals may wish to ensure they're fully informed about the changing legal requirements. Several training options exist to help stakeholders navigate these reforms, ranging from free introductory courses to bespoke in-house sessions.

Free and Paid Learning Resources

For those new to renting regulations or needing a refresher, a free 12-part online course covers essential landlord and tenant law. More comprehensive training comes through a series of 16 monthly webinars addressing multiple aspects of property letting, including:

  • Pre-tenancy preparations
  • Property standards compliance
  • Local authority enforcement procedures
  • Tenancy agreement best practices
  • Possession proceedings guidance

These live sessions allow for participant questions and offer recorded access for twelve months afterwards. While included in certain membership packages, non-members might consider paying £18 per webinar, with continuing professional development certificates available upon request.

Specialist Training Solutions

A three-hour masterclass recording remains accessible through business membership portals, detailing all Renters Reform Act changes. Organisations requiring tailored training might explore customised programmes delivered either by housing law specialists or qualified barristers.

From fundamental courses to specialist sessions, these learning opportunities could help property professionals maintain compliance and confidence during this period of legislative change.

item.title: MPs urge government to accelerate and strengthen leasehold reform
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs push for stronger leasehold reforms

Cross-party MPs have urged the government to bolster its proposed leasehold reforms, arguing current plans don’t go far enough to protect homeowners. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee’s report welcomes progress in the draft Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill but highlights key areas requiring tougher action.

Key recommendations for reform

The committee’s proposals focus on three main areas:

  • Ground rent cap: While supporting the proposed £250 annual cap, MPs recommend accelerating implementation to 2027 rather than the government’s suggested timeline.
  • Faster transition periods: The report questions why ground rents should take 40 years to phase out completely when a 20-year transition might better balance interests between leaseholders and investors.
  • Property management regulation: The committee calls for establishing an independent regulator with powers to sanction poor-performing agents, including revoking operating licences.

Addressing long-standing issues

The report criticises historical failures in addressing unfair leasehold practices, noting many homeowners face excessive service charges and limited control over their properties. Committee chair Florence Eshalomi MP stated the reforms represent progress but emphasised the need for stronger consumer protections against unreasonable fees.

With leasehold reform now moving through Parliament, homeowners may wish to monitor developments as these changes could significantly affect property ownership costs and rights in coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs push for stronger leasehold reforms

Cross-party MPs have urged the government to bolster its proposed leasehold reforms, arguing current plans don’t go far enough to protect homeowners. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee’s report welcomes progress in the draft Commonhold and Leasehold Reform Bill but highlights key areas requiring tougher action.

Key recommendations for reform

The committee’s proposals focus on three main areas:

  • Ground rent cap: While supporting the proposed £250 annual cap, MPs recommend accelerating implementation to 2027 rather than the government’s suggested timeline.
  • Faster transition periods: The report questions why ground rents should take 40 years to phase out completely when a 20-year transition might better balance interests between leaseholders and investors.
  • Property management regulation: The committee calls for establishing an independent regulator with powers to sanction poor-performing agents, including revoking operating licences.

Addressing long-standing issues

The report criticises historical failures in addressing unfair leasehold practices, noting many homeowners face excessive service charges and limited control over their properties. Committee chair Florence Eshalomi MP stated the reforms represent progress but emphasised the need for stronger consumer protections against unreasonable fees.

With leasehold reform now moving through Parliament, homeowners may wish to monitor developments as these changes could significantly affect property ownership costs and rights in coming years.

item.title: Rogue agent and landlord ordered to repay more than £90k
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A landlord and property management firm in east London have been ordered to repay over £90,000 following a council investigation into dangerous living conditions and unlawful activities at a rental home in Wanstead. The substantial repayment was issued under the Proceeds of Crime Act after authorities discovered multiple breaches at a five-bedroom property, including alleged drug cultivation and illegal electricity bypassing.

Council investigation uncovers multiple hazards

Redbridge Council launched an investigation following reports about the property, with Metropolitan Police later confirming criminal activity at the address. Initial inspections revealed the house empty but in poor condition, prompting officials to require remedial works before legal occupation. However, subsequent visits uncovered ten unrelated men living at the property despite its unsafe state.

Housing enforcement officers identified five category 1 hazards – the most serious classification under the Housing Act 2004 – alongside numerous other safety concerns. The council issued a Prohibition Order banning occupancy until repairs were completed, yet the landlord and agent allegedly continued renting the property for over a year in breach of this order.

Zero tolerance approach to rogue operators

The case reached Snaresbrook Crown Court, where property owner Chad Miah and All Season Lettings and Management faced proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act. The court ordered them to pay £91,068 in combined fines, costs and confiscation orders.

Councillor Saima Ahmed, Redbridge Council's cabinet member for housing, emphasised their strict stance: "This outcome demonstrates our commitment to protecting residents from exploitative practices. While we support responsible landlords, we'll use the full force of the law against those who endanger tenants for profit."

The council highlights this case as part of wider efforts to improve standards in the private rented sector. Authorities encourage tenants to report poor conditions through official channels, with enforcement teams prioritising investigations into the most serious violations.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A landlord and property management firm in east London have been ordered to repay over £90,000 following a council investigation into dangerous living conditions and unlawful activities at a rental home in Wanstead. The substantial repayment was issued under the Proceeds of Crime Act after authorities discovered multiple breaches at a five-bedroom property, including alleged drug cultivation and illegal electricity bypassing.

Council investigation uncovers multiple hazards

Redbridge Council launched an investigation following reports about the property, with Metropolitan Police later confirming criminal activity at the address. Initial inspections revealed the house empty but in poor condition, prompting officials to require remedial works before legal occupation. However, subsequent visits uncovered ten unrelated men living at the property despite its unsafe state.

Housing enforcement officers identified five category 1 hazards – the most serious classification under the Housing Act 2004 – alongside numerous other safety concerns. The council issued a Prohibition Order banning occupancy until repairs were completed, yet the landlord and agent allegedly continued renting the property for over a year in breach of this order.

Zero tolerance approach to rogue operators

The case reached Snaresbrook Crown Court, where property owner Chad Miah and All Season Lettings and Management faced proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act. The court ordered them to pay £91,068 in combined fines, costs and confiscation orders.

Councillor Saima Ahmed, Redbridge Council's cabinet member for housing, emphasised their strict stance: "This outcome demonstrates our commitment to protecting residents from exploitative practices. While we support responsible landlords, we'll use the full force of the law against those who endanger tenants for profit."

The council highlights this case as part of wider efforts to improve standards in the private rented sector. Authorities encourage tenants to report poor conditions through official channels, with enforcement teams prioritising investigations into the most serious violations.

item.title: Gradual Commonhold reform key to safeguarding housing supply
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Balancing Commonhold Reform With Housing Supply Needs

The government's draft legislation to reform leasehold ownership marks a significant shift in property law, with plans to transition new developments to commonhold arrangements. Whilst this fulfils a manifesto commitment to abolish leasehold, experts suggest the changes must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting already sluggish housing delivery targets.

The ambitious pledge to build 1.5 million homes during this Parliament comes at a time when housebuilding rates hover near historic lows. Achieving this target may require greater urban density developments, where flats often represent the only viable solution in land-constrained areas. The success of such schemes could partially depend on property developers' willingness to embrace commonhold models.

Overcoming the Challenges of Commonhold Adoption

Commonhold remains relatively untested across England and Wales, with fewer than 25 developments currently operating under this system. Industry stakeholders have expressed concerns about potential risks, given the limited track record for lenders, investors and developers. The government acknowledges that existing commonhold legislation, introduced in 2004, requires substantial refinement before widespread adoption becomes practical.

Key technical issues requiring attention include:

  • Effective dispute resolution mechanisms
  • Clear processes for amending constitutional documents
  • Provisions for terminating commonhold arrangements
  • Mortgage lender acceptance criteria

As the property market prepares for these changes, gradual implementation may prove crucial to maintaining housing supply whilst allowing time for the commonhold system to develop necessary safeguards. Homeowners considering new-build properties might wish to monitor how these reforms progress, as they could significantly influence future property ownership structures.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Balancing Commonhold Reform With Housing Supply Needs

The government's draft legislation to reform leasehold ownership marks a significant shift in property law, with plans to transition new developments to commonhold arrangements. Whilst this fulfils a manifesto commitment to abolish leasehold, experts suggest the changes must be carefully managed to avoid disrupting already sluggish housing delivery targets.

The ambitious pledge to build 1.5 million homes during this Parliament comes at a time when housebuilding rates hover near historic lows. Achieving this target may require greater urban density developments, where flats often represent the only viable solution in land-constrained areas. The success of such schemes could partially depend on property developers' willingness to embrace commonhold models.

Overcoming the Challenges of Commonhold Adoption

Commonhold remains relatively untested across England and Wales, with fewer than 25 developments currently operating under this system. Industry stakeholders have expressed concerns about potential risks, given the limited track record for lenders, investors and developers. The government acknowledges that existing commonhold legislation, introduced in 2004, requires substantial refinement before widespread adoption becomes practical.

Key technical issues requiring attention include:

  • Effective dispute resolution mechanisms
  • Clear processes for amending constitutional documents
  • Provisions for terminating commonhold arrangements
  • Mortgage lender acceptance criteria

As the property market prepares for these changes, gradual implementation may prove crucial to maintaining housing supply whilst allowing time for the commonhold system to develop necessary safeguards. Homeowners considering new-build properties might wish to monitor how these reforms progress, as they could significantly influence future property ownership structures.

item.title: BTL landlords see sharp rise in rental income despite widespread arrears
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords See Strong Rental Growth Despite Ongoing Arrears Challenges

New research reveals buy-to-let landlords have enjoyed significant rental income growth over the past year, even as many continue to grapple with tenant arrears. The latest market analysis shows average gross rental income per property surged by 22.9% year-on-year, climbing from £9,860 in early 2025 to £12,117 in Q1 2026.

The data highlights considerable regional variations in portfolio performance. The West Midlands emerged as a standout region, recording the strongest growth in both portfolio size and value. Landlords in this area increased their holdings by an average of 3.7 properties, while portfolio values jumped 29.4% to £2.2 million.

Regional Variations in Portfolio Performance

Whilst some regions flourished, others faced declines. Central London and the North East saw the sharpest reductions in average portfolio sizes, each dropping by 1.9 properties compared to the previous year. Portfolio values also fell noticeably in the North West, Yorkshire and Humber, and the South West.

Rental income growth proved particularly robust in the North West, where average income per property leapt by over 52%. The West Midlands, Wales, East of England and Central London also posted strong gains. Only the North East recorded a decrease in rental income per property.

With approximately 2.8 million landlords operating across the UK, the combined value of landlord portfolios now stands at an estimated £4.8 trillion. The average landlord owns 7.3 properties worth around £1.7 million.

Arrears Remain Persistent Challenge

Behind these positive headline figures lies an ongoing challenge for many landlords. Around 30% – equating to roughly 846,000 property owners – have experienced tenant arrears at some point during the past year.

Industry experts suggest the market presents a complex picture for landlords. Whilst rising rents reflect sustained tenant demand and constrained supply, many property investors simultaneously face higher borrowing costs and arrears pressures.

For those considering buy-to-let investments, these findings highlight the importance of thorough research into local market conditions and careful financial planning to account for potential voids or arrears periods.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords See Strong Rental Growth Despite Ongoing Arrears Challenges

New research reveals buy-to-let landlords have enjoyed significant rental income growth over the past year, even as many continue to grapple with tenant arrears. The latest market analysis shows average gross rental income per property surged by 22.9% year-on-year, climbing from £9,860 in early 2025 to £12,117 in Q1 2026.

The data highlights considerable regional variations in portfolio performance. The West Midlands emerged as a standout region, recording the strongest growth in both portfolio size and value. Landlords in this area increased their holdings by an average of 3.7 properties, while portfolio values jumped 29.4% to £2.2 million.

Regional Variations in Portfolio Performance

Whilst some regions flourished, others faced declines. Central London and the North East saw the sharpest reductions in average portfolio sizes, each dropping by 1.9 properties compared to the previous year. Portfolio values also fell noticeably in the North West, Yorkshire and Humber, and the South West.

Rental income growth proved particularly robust in the North West, where average income per property leapt by over 52%. The West Midlands, Wales, East of England and Central London also posted strong gains. Only the North East recorded a decrease in rental income per property.

With approximately 2.8 million landlords operating across the UK, the combined value of landlord portfolios now stands at an estimated £4.8 trillion. The average landlord owns 7.3 properties worth around £1.7 million.

Arrears Remain Persistent Challenge

Behind these positive headline figures lies an ongoing challenge for many landlords. Around 30% – equating to roughly 846,000 property owners – have experienced tenant arrears at some point during the past year.

Industry experts suggest the market presents a complex picture for landlords. Whilst rising rents reflect sustained tenant demand and constrained supply, many property investors simultaneously face higher borrowing costs and arrears pressures.

For those considering buy-to-let investments, these findings highlight the importance of thorough research into local market conditions and careful financial planning to account for potential voids or arrears periods.

item.title: Government-backed property data trial tests future of homebuying
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A pioneering government-backed trial has explored how secure property data sharing could transform the homebuying process in England and Wales. The Smart Property Data Trust Framework sandbox, funded by a £742,700 grant from the Regulators’ Pioneer Fund, has tested new approaches to verifying and exchanging housing information between approved parties.

Streamlining property transactions

The 12-month initiative, led by the Council for Licensed Conveyancers in partnership with the Open Property Data Association (OPDA), created a controlled environment for testing shared data standards. Unlike current systems that rely on manual document handling or bespoke integrations between individual firms, the framework allows multiple organisations to connect through a single, reusable system.

PropTech firm Kotini participated as an early tester, accessing HM Land Registry data through the sandbox. Founder Kieran Witt described how the traditional approach created unnecessary barriers: "Each new integration required significant time and resources - what we called an 'innovation tax'. This framework removes those repetitive technical hurdles, letting firms focus on improving services rather than administrative overheads."

Potential industry-wide benefits

The trial demonstrated how verified property data could flow securely between different market participants while maintaining clear records of its origin. Maria Harris, OPDA chair, highlighted the collaborative nature of the project: "The sandbox supports the whole ecosystem working together. As it's not a closed environment, any approved organisation can participate in testing data sharing."

Key advantages identified during the trial include:

  • Reduced duplication when accessing core property information
  • Standardised approaches to data verification
  • Potential cost savings across the transaction chain
  • Improved accessibility for smaller property firms

Whilst the sandbox continues operating until later this year, early results suggest such frameworks could help modernise the property market. Homeowners may find future transactions become more efficient as these data-sharing standards develop, potentially reducing delays and paperwork in the homebuying process.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A pioneering government-backed trial has explored how secure property data sharing could transform the homebuying process in England and Wales. The Smart Property Data Trust Framework sandbox, funded by a £742,700 grant from the Regulators’ Pioneer Fund, has tested new approaches to verifying and exchanging housing information between approved parties.

Streamlining property transactions

The 12-month initiative, led by the Council for Licensed Conveyancers in partnership with the Open Property Data Association (OPDA), created a controlled environment for testing shared data standards. Unlike current systems that rely on manual document handling or bespoke integrations between individual firms, the framework allows multiple organisations to connect through a single, reusable system.

PropTech firm Kotini participated as an early tester, accessing HM Land Registry data through the sandbox. Founder Kieran Witt described how the traditional approach created unnecessary barriers: "Each new integration required significant time and resources - what we called an 'innovation tax'. This framework removes those repetitive technical hurdles, letting firms focus on improving services rather than administrative overheads."

Potential industry-wide benefits

The trial demonstrated how verified property data could flow securely between different market participants while maintaining clear records of its origin. Maria Harris, OPDA chair, highlighted the collaborative nature of the project: "The sandbox supports the whole ecosystem working together. As it's not a closed environment, any approved organisation can participate in testing data sharing."

Key advantages identified during the trial include:

  • Reduced duplication when accessing core property information
  • Standardised approaches to data verification
  • Potential cost savings across the transaction chain
  • Improved accessibility for smaller property firms

Whilst the sandbox continues operating until later this year, early results suggest such frameworks could help modernise the property market. Homeowners may find future transactions become more efficient as these data-sharing standards develop, potentially reducing delays and paperwork in the homebuying process.

item.title: Estate agent turned politician accuses Andy Burnham of hypocrisy over let flat
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs' Property Investments Under Scrutiny

The relationship between politicians and property ownership has come under fresh scrutiny following revelations about Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham’s London flat. The Labour figure purchased the ex-council property in Kennington during his time as an MP in 2005, with reports suggesting partial funding came through parliamentary expenses. The flat, now valued at approximately £480,000, has been rented out whilst Burnham claimed accommodation expenses for another property.

Housing Market Hypocrisy Claims

Conservative MP and former Hunters estate agency founder Kevin Hollinrake has criticised Burnham’s position, labelling it hypocritical given his public stance on housing affordability. Burnham has frequently spoken about growing wealth disparities between property owners and those struggling to enter the market. Hollinrake remarked: "The self-styled King of the North maintains a profitable London property portfolio whilst criticising the very system that enabled his investment."

This situation echoes the 2012 controversy when Burnham, then shadow health secretary, faced questions about claiming rental expenses whilst letting out his own property. The parliamentary expenses system underwent significant reform after the 2009 scandal, prohibiting MPs from claiming mortgage interest but still allowing London accommodation costs.

Right to Buy Paradox

The debate touches upon wider housing policy contradictions. Burnham has advocated for rent controls and called to suspend Right to Buy – the scheme allowing council tenants to purchase their homes at discounts. Ironically, his Kennington flat was originally acquired through Right to Buy before he purchased it. Housing analysts suggest such cases highlight the complex relationship individuals can have with property markets, where personal investments may not always align with public policy positions.

As housing remains a contentious political issue, the financial decisions of policymakers continue to attract public interest and scrutiny. Homeowners considering buy-to-let investments might reflect on how market participation intersects with broader housing affordability debates.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs' Property Investments Under Scrutiny

The relationship between politicians and property ownership has come under fresh scrutiny following revelations about Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham’s London flat. The Labour figure purchased the ex-council property in Kennington during his time as an MP in 2005, with reports suggesting partial funding came through parliamentary expenses. The flat, now valued at approximately £480,000, has been rented out whilst Burnham claimed accommodation expenses for another property.

Housing Market Hypocrisy Claims

Conservative MP and former Hunters estate agency founder Kevin Hollinrake has criticised Burnham’s position, labelling it hypocritical given his public stance on housing affordability. Burnham has frequently spoken about growing wealth disparities between property owners and those struggling to enter the market. Hollinrake remarked: "The self-styled King of the North maintains a profitable London property portfolio whilst criticising the very system that enabled his investment."

This situation echoes the 2012 controversy when Burnham, then shadow health secretary, faced questions about claiming rental expenses whilst letting out his own property. The parliamentary expenses system underwent significant reform after the 2009 scandal, prohibiting MPs from claiming mortgage interest but still allowing London accommodation costs.

Right to Buy Paradox

The debate touches upon wider housing policy contradictions. Burnham has advocated for rent controls and called to suspend Right to Buy – the scheme allowing council tenants to purchase their homes at discounts. Ironically, his Kennington flat was originally acquired through Right to Buy before he purchased it. Housing analysts suggest such cases highlight the complex relationship individuals can have with property markets, where personal investments may not always align with public policy positions.

As housing remains a contentious political issue, the financial decisions of policymakers continue to attract public interest and scrutiny. Homeowners considering buy-to-let investments might reflect on how market participation intersects with broader housing affordability debates.

item.title: Propertymark welcomes leasehold ban proposal
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Potential leasehold ban could reshape flat ownership

The government's proposals to ban leasehold flats in England and Wales have gained support from industry experts, who suggest the move could create a fairer system for homeowners. Under the plans, most new-build flats would adopt a commonhold ownership model rather than traditional leasehold arrangements. This structural shift might give flat owners more control over building management whilst eliminating recurring costs like ground rent.

Why leasehold reform is being considered

Industry research indicates widespread dissatisfaction with leasehold properties, with over 90% of owners expressing regret about their purchase. Common concerns include unpredictable service charges, restrictive lease terms and opaque management practices. Estate agents report increasing difficulties in selling leasehold flats, suggesting the current model may be deterring potential buyers. The proposed reforms could make flat ownership more attractive to first-time buyers and property investors alike.

However, transition challenges must be carefully managed to avoid unintended consequences for existing leaseholders. Property professionals caution against mandatory conversion requirements that might create financial pressures. Instead, they advocate for phased implementation with proper protections for current homeowners. Clear guidance for buyers about commonhold obligations and rights would also be crucial as this alternative model becomes more prevalent.

The road ahead for property reforms

Alongside ownership structure changes, industry bodies are calling for tighter regulation of managing agents to improve transparency. These combined measures could help address systemic issues while maintaining market confidence. Homeowners considering purchasing leasehold properties might wish to research proposed changes carefully, whilst existing leaseholders may benefit from monitoring how protections for their situations develop. The government's consultation period offers an opportunity for stakeholders to shape this significant housing policy shift.

As these discussions progress, the property market could witness one of its most substantial transformations in decades – potentially redefining what flat ownership means for future generations of homeowners.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Potential leasehold ban could reshape flat ownership

The government's proposals to ban leasehold flats in England and Wales have gained support from industry experts, who suggest the move could create a fairer system for homeowners. Under the plans, most new-build flats would adopt a commonhold ownership model rather than traditional leasehold arrangements. This structural shift might give flat owners more control over building management whilst eliminating recurring costs like ground rent.

Why leasehold reform is being considered

Industry research indicates widespread dissatisfaction with leasehold properties, with over 90% of owners expressing regret about their purchase. Common concerns include unpredictable service charges, restrictive lease terms and opaque management practices. Estate agents report increasing difficulties in selling leasehold flats, suggesting the current model may be deterring potential buyers. The proposed reforms could make flat ownership more attractive to first-time buyers and property investors alike.

However, transition challenges must be carefully managed to avoid unintended consequences for existing leaseholders. Property professionals caution against mandatory conversion requirements that might create financial pressures. Instead, they advocate for phased implementation with proper protections for current homeowners. Clear guidance for buyers about commonhold obligations and rights would also be crucial as this alternative model becomes more prevalent.

The road ahead for property reforms

Alongside ownership structure changes, industry bodies are calling for tighter regulation of managing agents to improve transparency. These combined measures could help address systemic issues while maintaining market confidence. Homeowners considering purchasing leasehold properties might wish to research proposed changes carefully, whilst existing leaseholders may benefit from monitoring how protections for their situations develop. The government's consultation period offers an opportunity for stakeholders to shape this significant housing policy shift.

As these discussions progress, the property market could witness one of its most substantial transformations in decades – potentially redefining what flat ownership means for future generations of homeowners.

item.title: Andy Burnham vs the bond market – why housing could feel the impact
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Political shifts and the UK property market

Recent proposals for an additional council tax levy on high-value properties owned by non-UK residents have sparked debate about the potential impact on the housing market. The measure, initially introduced in November's Budget for properties valued over £2 million, could form part of broader political positioning as speculation grows about future leadership challenges within the Labour party.

Manchester mayor Andy Burnham's campaign for a parliamentary seat has brought attention to potential policy directions, with observers noting his historical preference for higher taxation and public spending. Whilst campaign rhetoric often differs from eventual policy, homeowners in premium markets might consider how political changes could influence property taxation and valuations.

International investment and prime market performance

The proposed surcharge has drawn criticism from business leaders who argue it sends mixed signals about the UK's openness to international investment. Leslie MacLeod-Miller of Foreign Investors for Britain suggests that existing measures, including high stamp duty rates and changes to non-dom status, already make the UK less competitive in attracting global wealth.

Data from prime central London appears to support these concerns, with LonRes figures showing an 18% decline in £5m+ property exchanges compared to the five-year average. Average prices in this segment have fallen 3.8% annually, continuing a three-year downward trend that leaves values 22% below their 2015 peak.

Balancing policy objectives

The debate highlights the challenge policymakers face in balancing revenue generation with maintaining the UK's appeal to high-net-worth individuals. Estate agents note that many overseas buyers in this price bracket are long-term residents who contribute significantly to local economies, rather than the absentee owners sometimes characterised in political discourse.

As political and economic landscapes evolve, those with interests in premium property markets might monitor how taxation policies develop, particularly in relation to international investment flows and domestic housing market performance.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Political shifts and the UK property market
